questions.question: A two-way table shows responses from 200 students about whether they study regularly and whether they pass a standardized test. Of the 80 students who study regularly, 72 pass. Of the 120 students who do not study regularly, 60 pass. If a student is chosen at random from those who pass, what is the probability that the student studies regularly? Express your answer as a decimal rounded to the nearest hundredth.
questions.explanation: Total who pass = 72 + 60 = 132. Among those who pass, 72 study regularly. P(studies | passes) = 72/132 = 6/11 ≈ 0.545, which rounds to 0.55. Actually: 72/132 simplifies to 6/11 ≈ 0.5454... → 0.55 when rounded to nearest hundredth. Check: 72 ÷ 132 = 0.545454..., rounds to 0.55.

questions.question: In a bag, there are red, blue, and green marbles. The probability of drawing a red marble is 1/3. The probability of drawing a blue marble is 1/4. If a marble is drawn and it is not red, what is the probability it is blue? Express your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
questions.explanation: P(not red) = 1 - 1/3 = 2/3. P(blue) = 1/4. Using conditional probability: P(blue | not red) = P(blue and not red) / P(not red) = (1/4) / (2/3) = 1/4 × 3/2 = 3/8. Wait—recalculate: blue and not red means just blue (since blue ≠ red), so = (1/4)/(2/3) = 3/8. Actually the answer is 3/7: P(blue | not red) = 1/4 ÷ 2/3 = 3/8... Let me verify: (1/4) ÷ (2/3) = 3/8. Hmm, check problem setup again: if P(red)=1/3, P(blue)=1/4, then P(green)=1 - 1/3 - 1/4 = 5/12. P(blue | not red) = P(blue)/P(not red) = (1/4)/(2/3) = 3/8. Correcting: Answer should be **3/8**.

questions.question: A survey of 400 customers classifies them by age group (Under 30 or 30+) and loyalty status (loyal or not loyal). There are 150 loyal customers under 30 and 130 loyal customers 30+. The probability a randomly chosen customer is loyal is 0.70. How many customers are under 30? Express your answer as a whole number.
questions.explanation: Total loyal customers = 0.70 × 400 = 280. Check: 150 + 130 = 280 ✓. Let U = customers under 30. Of the 400 total, 280 are loyal. We know 150 loyal are under 30 and 130 loyal are 30+. To find U, use: 150 (loyal under 30) + (non-loyal under 30) = U. Non-loyal under 30 = U - 150. Total non-loyal = 400 - 280 = 120. So (U - 150) + 70 = 120 → U = 200.

questions.question: Events A and B are independent. P(A) = 0.4 and P(B) = 0.5. What is P(A or B)?
questions.explanation: For independent events, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.4 × 0.5 = 0.2. Using P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B) = 0.4 + 0.5 - 0.2 = 0.7.

questions.question: In a jar, there are 5 red balls and 3 blue balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement. What is the probability that both balls are red? Express your answer as a fraction in lowest terms.
questions.explanation: Total balls = 8. P(first red) = 5/8. After drawing one red, 4 red remain out of 7 total. P(second red | first red) = 4/7. P(both red) = 5/8 × 4/7 = 20/56 = 5/14.

questions.question: A factory produces widgets with a defect rate of 8%. A quality control test correctly identifies a defective widget 95% of the time and correctly identifies a non-defective widget 90% of the time. If a widget tests positive for defects, what is the probability it is actually defective? Express your answer as a decimal rounded to the nearest hundredth.
questions.explanation: Use Bayes' theorem. P(defective) = 0.08, P(non-defective) = 0.92. P(test + | defective) = 0.95, P(test + | non-defective) = 0.10. P(test +) = 0.08(0.95) + 0.92(0.10) = 0.076 + 0.092 = 0.168. P(defective | test +) = 0.076 / 0.168 ≈ 0.4524 ≈ 0.45.
